summary: Gruber analyzes Jeff Williams's exceptional performance as Apple's SVP of Operations, noting the record 74 million iPhones sold in Q1 2015 with minimal supply constraints. He broadens the discussion to Apple CEO succession planning, arguing that any replacement for Tim Cook would come from within the company's existing leadership. Gruber identifies Eddy Cue, Jony Ive, Phil Schiller, and Williams as the current short list, while emphasizing this is highly speculative since Cook is only 53 and performing exceptionally. He makes the interesting observation that if Cook stays another 15 years, his eventual successor is likely someone not yet known to the public. The piece reflects on how Apple's institutional culture, codified through Apple University, makes internal succession the only realistic path.
key_points: Jeff Williams deserves credit for the operational achievement of having 74 million iPhones available to sell in a single quarter, up from 10 million for all of 2008
Williams's responsibilities extend beyond iPhone manufacturing to all operations company-wide, plus overseeing Apple Watch development
Apple's board would not consider an outside CEO candidate unless the company falters significantly, due to the codified 'Apple Way' of doing things
The current short list of Cook successors is Cue, Ive, Schiller, and Williams, with Ahrendts too new and Ive least likely to want the job
If Cook remains CEO until roughly 2030, his successor will likely be someone not yet publicly known who rises through the ranks
money_quotes: All credit to the hardware, software, and product marketing teams for the fact that 74 million people wanted to buy an iPhone last quarter. But the credit goes to Williams's operations team that there were 74 million units available to sell.
For context, in 2008, Apple sold a total 10 million iPhones for the entire year.
There is an Apple Way of doing things — codified, documented, and taught to employees as they move into management through the company's Joel Podolny-led Apple University.
I doubt Apple's board would consider an outsider as CEO until and unless the company falters significantly and loses its way.
What makes this speculation fun is that at the moment, at least from our perspective outside the company, there is no clear Number Two being groomed.
But a replacement who isn't named until Tim Cook's retirement in, say, 2030, will likely be someone we don't yet know — someone who'll rise through the company's ranks between now and then.
key_insight: Apple's institutional culture and internal leadership development make outside CEO candidates virtually unthinkable, but the current leadership team's ages mean Cook's eventual successor may be someone not yet publicly known.
